title: Deep Console Table with Hutch
summary: A turned-leg console table with an open-back hutch riser on top — extra display and storage height without extending the table's footprint.
description:
Building on turned legs rather than square stock skips the joinery entirely for the base — the legs come with their own attachment hardware, and the real work is the apron frame that ties them together and the tabletop that sits on it. Adding extra support boards underneath the tabletop, beyond what the apron alone provides, is worth doing even though it's optional in the source: a deep tabletop spanning this kind of length benefits from the extra backing, especially once the hutch's weight sits on top of it.

The hutch is really its own small shelf unit that happens to sit on the console rather than the wall — it's attached from underneath with screws, so it can be removed later if the piece ever needs to be used as a plain console table instead.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Or a pine project panel for the tabletop.

steps.title: Build the table base
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes facing upward on the aprons before attaching them to the legs — much easier to drill flat on the bench than after the base is assembled. Attach the aprons to the turned legs to form the basic table shape.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop
steps.body: Attach the tabletop to the aprons using the predrilled 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Add extra support under the top
steps.body: Add extra support boards underneath the tabletop beyond the apron alone — a deep top spanning this length benefits from it, especially once the hutch's weight sits on top.

steps.title: Attach the stretchers
steps.body: Attach stretchers between the legs at the stretcher blocks, hiding the 3/4" pocket holes on the underside.

steps.title: Build the hutch
steps.body: Attach the hutch's back boards to the dividers with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Attach the hutch top
steps.body: Attach the top to the hutch framing with nails and glue.

steps.title: Attach the hutch to the table
steps.body:
Attach the completed hutch to the tabletop from underneath with screws — this keeps the connection removable if the piece ever needs to work as a plain console table.

Sand every surface and finish.

images.alt: Deep console table with an open hutch riser on top
